Block 5 · Head & Neck

LO 43C — Ascending Arteries to the Head

Overview Common Carotid External Carotid Internal Carotid Vertebral Arteries Circle of Willis Gallery
LO 43C

Ascending Arteries to the Head: Origin, Course & Branches

The brain and scalp receive their blood supply from two paired systems that ascend through the neck: the carotid arteries (anterior circulation) and the vertebral arteries (posterior circulation). These anastomose at the base of the brain via the circle of Willis (cerebral arterial circle), providing critical collateral protection.

Anterior Circulation

  • Common carotid artery → bifurcates at C4 level into:
  • External carotid — 8 named branches to face/scalp/viscera
  • Internal carotid — 3 major branches to brain/orbit

Posterior Circulation

  • Vertebral artery — 1st branch of subclavian artery
  • Ascends through foramina transversaria C6→C1
  • Unites to form basilar artery → cerebral branches
COA Fig 9.15 Dissections of anterior cervical and suprahyoid regions — carotid systems in situ Moore's Clinically Oriented Anatomy, 8e
COA Fig 9.16 Deep dissection of anterior cervical region showing carotid bifurcation and branches Moore's Clinically Oriented Anatomy, 8e
Common Carotid Arteries

Origin & Course of the Common Carotid Arteries

Right Common Carotid (CCA)

Arises from the bifurcation of the brachiocephalic trunk (innominate artery), posterior to the right sternoclavicular joint. The brachiocephalic trunk itself arises from the aortic arch.

Left Common Carotid (CCA)

Arises directly from the aortic arch as the second branch, between the brachiocephalic trunk and the left subclavian artery. Has a short thoracic course before entering the neck.

Course

Both CCAs ascend within the carotid sheath alongside the internal jugular vein (lateral) and vagus nerve (posterior). They pass deep to the sternocleidomastoid and give no named branches in the neck until they bifurcate.

Aortic arch / Brachiocephalic → CCA ascends in carotid sheath → Bifurcation at C4 (upper border of thyroid cartilage) → ECA + ICA
🏥 Carotid Sinus & Carotid Body
The carotid sinus is a dilation at the base of the ICA (or CCA bifurcation) — it is a baroreceptor sensitive to arterial blood pressure (innervated by CN IX, the glossopharyngeal nerve). The carotid body is a small chemoreceptor organ nestled in the bifurcation angle, sensitive to O₂, CO₂, and pH (also CN IX + CN X). Pressure on the carotid sinus can cause reflex bradycardia and syncope.
COA Fig 9.6 Carotid sheath and its neurovascular contents Moore's Clinically Oriented Anatomy, 8e
COA Fig 9.18 Carotid body and carotid sinus at the bifurcation Moore's Clinically Oriented Anatomy, 8e
COA Fig 9.20 Subclavian and carotid arteries and their branches — overview Moore's Clinically Oriented Anatomy, 8e
External Carotid Artery

External Carotid Artery — 8 Major Branches

The ECA arises at the carotid bifurcation (C4) and ascends in the anterior triangle, passing deep to the posterior belly of digastric and stylohyoid muscles to enter the parotid gland, where it terminates by dividing into the maxillary and superficial temporal arteries.

🗝️ Mnemonic — 8 Branches of the ECA
Some Anatomists Like Freaking Out Poor Medical Students
Superior thyroid · Ascending pharyngeal · Lingual · Facial · Occipital · Posterior auricular · Maxillary · Superficial temporal

Branches by Group

Anterior Branches (3)

  • 1
    Superior Thyroid A.
    1st branch of ECA. Descends to supply upper pole of thyroid gland; gives off superior laryngeal branch (with internal branch of SLN).
  • 2
    Lingual A.
    Arises at level of hyoid bone. Passes deep to hypoglossal nerve and posterior digastric; supplies tongue, floor of mouth, and sublingual gland via deep lingual and sublingual branches.
  • 3
    Facial A.
    Arises just above lingual (or common trunk). Deep course through submandibular gland, then hooks over mandible to supply face. Terminal branch = angular artery at medial canthus of eye.

Posterior Branches (2)

  • 5
    Occipital A.
    Arises opposite facial A. Passes posteriorly deep to posterior belly of digastric; emerges between trapezius and SCM to supply posterior scalp. Gives off mastoid and meningeal branches.
  • 6
    Posterior Auricular A.
    Small branch above occipital A; supplies auricle, scalp posterior to ear, parotid gland, and facial nerve within the parotid. Gives stylomastoid branch (supplies middle ear).

Medial Branch (1)

  • 4
    Ascending Pharyngeal A.
    Smallest and most medial branch. Arises near origin of ECA, ascends on pharyngeal wall. Supplies pharynx, prevertebral muscles, and middle ear (inferior tympanic branch). Also gives posterior meningeal branch.

Terminal Branches (2)

  • 7
    Superficial Temporal A.
    One of two terminal branches. Exits parotid gland, crosses zygomatic arch, and divides into frontal and parietal branches to supply the lateral scalp. The transverse facial branch arises here to supply the cheek. Palpable anterior to tragus of ear — clinically important in temporal arteritis.
  • 8
    Maxillary A.
    The larger terminal branch. Passes deep to the neck of the mandible and through the infratemporal fossa. Has 3 parts and numerous branches (see below). Key branches include the middle meningeal artery (supply dura, at risk in pterion fracture), inferior alveolar A., deep temporal arteries, and sphenopalatine A. (terminal — epistaxis)
⚠️ Middle Meningeal Artery — Clinically Vital
Branch of the 1st part of the maxillary artery. Enters the skull via the foramen spinosum. Runs in the epidural space in a groove on the inner table of the temporal bone. Rupture from a blow to the pterion (thinnest part of skull) causes an epidural hematoma — "lucid interval" then rapid deterioration as blood accumulates and herniates the uncus.

Maxillary Artery — 3 Parts & Key Branches

PartLocationKey BranchesTerritory
1st (Mandibular)Between mandible and sphenomandibular ligamentMiddle meningeal A., Deep auricular A., Anterior tympanic A., Inferior alveolar A.Dura, ear, lower teeth
2nd (Pterygoid)Superficial to lower head of lateral pterygoidDeep temporal AA., Pterygoid branches, Masseteric A., Buccal A.Muscles of mastication
3rd (Pterygopalatine)Pterygopalatine fossaPosterior superior alveolar, Infraorbital A., Palatine branches, Sphenopalatine A.Upper teeth, palate, nasal cavity
COA Fig 8.24 Superficial arteries of the face and scalp — branches of the external carotid artery Moore's Clinically Oriented Anatomy, 8e
COA Fig 8.73 Parts and branches of the maxillary artery (3 parts: mandibular, pterygoid, pterygopalatine) Moore's Clinically Oriented Anatomy, 8e
Internal Carotid Artery

Internal Carotid Artery — Course & 3 Major Branches

The ICA arises at the carotid bifurcation (C4) and gives NO branches in the neck. It ascends in the carotid sheath to enter the skull via the carotid canal in the petrous temporal bone, then traverses the cavernous sinus (forming the carotid siphon), and enters the subarachnoid space to give its cerebral branches.

Parts of the ICA (C1–C7 segments)

C1 Cervical → C2 Petrous (carotid canal) → C3 Lacerum → C4 Cavernous (siphon) → C5 Clinoid → C6 Ophthalmic → C7 Communicating
🔬 Carotid Siphon
In the cavernous sinus, the ICA makes an S-shaped curve (the "carotid siphon") — visible on lateral arteriograms. The ICA exits the sinus by piercing the dural roof medial to the anterior clinoid process and enters the subarachnoid space. CN III, IV, V₁, V₂, and VI all run in the walls of the cavernous sinus — so cavernous sinus thrombosis or an ICA aneurysm here can damage all these nerves simultaneously.

3 Major Branches of the ICA

  • 1
    Ophthalmic Artery
    Arises as the ICA exits the cavernous sinus, just medial to the anterior clinoid process. Passes through the optic canal with CN II (optic nerve). Supplies the orbit, eye, and gives off:
    — Central retinal artery (end-artery; retinal ischemia = sudden painless vision loss)
    — Posterior and anterior ciliary arteries (supply choroid and anterior segment)
    — Supratrochlear, supraorbital, dorsal nasal branches (anastomose with facial artery — danger triangle)
  • 2
    Posterior Communicating Artery (PCommA)
    Runs posteriorly from the ICA to anastomose with the posterior cerebral artery (PCA), forming part of the circle of Willis. Important: CN III (oculomotor nerve) runs alongside the PCommA — aneurysm of the PCommA classically presents as a painful CN III palsy with a dilated, unreactive pupil (compression of the peripheral parasympathetic fibers on the outside of CN III).
  • 3
    Anterior Choroidal Artery
    Arises from the ICA just distal to the PCommA. Passes posteriorly through the choroidal fissure into the temporal horn of the lateral ventricle. Supplies the choroid plexus, optic tract, lateral geniculate nucleus, posterior limb of internal capsule (fibers for motor and sensory function), and part of the cerebral peduncle. Occlusion causes a devastating triad: contralateral hemiplegia, hemianesthesia, and homonymous hemianopsia.

Terminal Branches (after ICA bifurcates)

Anterior Cerebral Artery (ACA)

Supplies the medial surface of the frontal and parietal lobes. The two ACAs are connected by the anterior communicating artery — the most common site of intracranial aneurysm. Occlusion causes contralateral leg weakness (leg area on medial cortex).

Middle Cerebral Artery (MCA)

Largest branch of the ICA. Runs in the lateral sulcus (Sylvian fissure) and supplies the lateral surface of the cerebral hemisphere including motor/sensory cortex for face and arm, Broca's and Wernicke's areas. Most common site of ischemic stroke.

COA Fig 8.40 Course of the internal carotid artery through petrous bone and cavernous sinus Moore's Clinically Oriented Anatomy, 8e
COA Fig 8.41 Carotid arteriograms showing the ICA, carotid siphon, and cerebral branches Moore's Clinically Oriented Anatomy, 8e
COA Fig 8.43 Arterial blood supply of the cerebrum — ACA, MCA, PCA territories Moore's Clinically Oriented Anatomy, 8e
Vertebral Arteries

Vertebral Arteries — Origin, Course & Branches

Origin & Parts

The vertebral artery (VA) is the first and largest branch of the subclavian artery. It has 4 parts:

V1 Prevertebral (subclavian → C6 transverse foramen) → V2 Foraminal (C6 → C2, through foramina transversaria) → V3 Atlantic (C2 → foramen magnum, around posterior arch of atlas) → V4 Intracranial (pierces dura → unites to form basilar A.)
🦴 Foramina Transversaria
The vertebral arteries pass through the foramina transversaria of C6 through C1 (not C7 — the VA typically passes anterior to C7, though it may occasionally enter at C7 as a variant). At C1 (atlas), the VA exits the foramen transversarium and winds around the posterior arch of the atlas in a groove (sulcus arteriosus) before piercing the posterior atlantooccipital membrane and dural sac to enter the foramen magnum. This segment is vulnerable to compression with extreme neck rotation (e.g., chiropractic manipulation → vertebrobasilar stroke).

Branches of the Vertebral Artery

BranchOriginTerritory / Notes
Spinal branchesV2 (foraminal part)Segmental arteries that enter intervertebral foramina; reinforce anterior & posterior spinal arteries
Muscular branchesV3 (atlantic part)Suboccipital and deep cervical muscles
Meningeal branchV4 (intracranial)Posterior cranial fossa dura mater
Posterior spinal artery (PSA)V4 — usually from PICADescends on posterior surface of spinal cord; supplies posterior one-third including dorsal columns and posterior horns
Anterior spinal artery (ASA)V4 — both VAs uniteSingle midline artery descending anterior to cord; supplies anterior two-thirds. Occlusion = anterior cord syndrome
Posterior Inferior Cerebellar A. (PICA)V4 — largest branch of VASupplies posterior inferior cerebellum, lateral medulla (including CN IX, X nuclei, spinothalamic tract, sympathetic fibers). Occlusion = lateral medullary syndrome (Wallenberg's). PICA also usually gives the PSA.

Basilar Artery — Formed by Vertebral Union

The two VAs unite at the pontomedullary junction to form the single basilar artery. The basilar artery ascends along the ventral surface of the pons and gives off:

🧠
Basilar Branch
Anterior Inferior Cerebellar A. (AICA)
Supplies anterior inferior cerebellum, lateral pons, inner ear (labyrinthine artery usually from AICA). Occlusion → AICA syndrome: ipsilateral facial weakness, hearing loss, vertigo
🔬
Basilar Branch
Pontine Arteries
Multiple short perforating branches to the pons (paramedian and circumferential). Occlusion of paramedian pontine branches → "locked-in syndrome" (basilar artery thrombosis)
🧬
Basilar Branch
Superior Cerebellar A. (SCA)
Just below the bifurcation. Supplies superior cerebellum, dorsal pons, choroid plexus of 4th ventricle. Closely related to CN III & IV at the midbrain
🔁
Terminal
Posterior Cerebral AA. (PCA)
Terminal bifurcation of basilar. Supplies occipital lobe (primary visual cortex), posterior thalamus, and medial temporal lobe. Communicates with ICA via PCommA to complete circle of Willis. Occlusion → contralateral homonymous hemianopsia
COA Fig 9.12 Subclavian artery — its three parts and branches, including the vertebral artery (1st branch) Moore's Clinically Oriented Anatomy, 8e
COA Fig 8.39 Arterial supply to the brain — vertebral arteries, basilar artery, and cerebral branches Moore's Clinically Oriented Anatomy, 8e
Circle of Willis

Cerebral Arterial Circle (Circle of Willis)

The circle of Willis is an anastomotic ring at the base of the brain formed by contributions from both carotid and both vertebral systems. It provides critical collateral blood flow when one vessel is occluded — though anatomic variations are extremely common (complete circle in only ~20–25% of people).

ACA (L) ↔ Ant. Communicating A. ↔ ACA (R)
ICA (L+R) ↔ Post. Communicating AA. ↔ PCA (L+R)
PCA (L) ↔ Basilar A. terminal bifurcation ↔ PCA (R)
ComponentArtery SystemNotes
Anterior communicating A.Both ICAs (via ACAs)Most common site of intracranial aneurysm; rupture → SAH with anterior interhemispheric blood
Anterior cerebral AA. (×2)ICA (terminal)Medial frontal/parietal; leg > arm > face representation
Internal carotid AA. (×2)CCAForm the lateral parts of the circle
Posterior communicating AA. (×2)ICA → PCA2nd most common aneurysm site; CN III palsy with dilated pupil
Posterior cerebral AA. (×2)Basilar (vertebral system)Occipital lobe (vision); thalamus; posterior temporal
⚠️ Aneurysm Locations — High-Yield Summary
  • Anterior communicating A. — most common (~30–35%); rupture causes SAH, blood in interhemispheric fissure
  • Posterior communicating A. — second most common; classically presents as CN III palsy (pupil-involving) before rupture
  • MCA bifurcation — third most common; temporal/Sylvian fissure SAH
  • Basilar tip / SCA origin — CN III compression from above; "top of the basilar" syndrome

Review

Self-Check MCQs

Five high-yield review questions on the ascending arteries to the head. Distractors are kept close in length so the answer is not visually telegraphed.

1. Which statement best distinguishes the internal carotid artery from the external carotid artery in the neck?
  • A. It gives no cervical branches before entering the carotid canal
  • B. It ends by dividing into facial and maxillary branches only
  • C. It passes through the foramen spinosum with middle meningeal flow
  • D. It supplies the thyroid gland through a superior thyroid branch
Reveal Answer

A. The internal carotid artery has no branches in the neck before entering the carotid canal.

2. A lesion at the carotid bifurcation would be found most classically at the level of which vertebra?
  • A. C2, near the superior extent of the pharyngeal wall
  • B. C4, near the upper border of the thyroid cartilage
  • C. C6, near the cricoid cartilage and oesophageal inlet
  • D. T1, near the root of the neck and pleural dome
Reveal Answer

B. The common carotid artery typically bifurcates around C4, close to the superior border of the thyroid cartilage.

3. Which branch of the subclavian artery ascends through the transverse foramina to help supply the brain?
  • A. Vertebral artery, ascending to the cranial cavity through cervical foramina
  • B. Lingual artery, ascending to the tongue through suprahyoid tissues
  • C. Facial artery, ascending to the face across the mandible
  • D. Maxillary artery, ascending to the infratemporal fossa through the jaw
Reveal Answer

A. The vertebral artery arises from the subclavian artery and ascends through the transverse foramina before entering the cranial cavity.

4. The maxillary artery is most accurately described as which vessel?
  • A. A terminal branch of external carotid deep to the neck of mandible
  • B. A terminal branch of internal carotid within the cavernous sinus
  • C. A branch of vertebral artery within the posterior cranial fossa
  • D. A branch of common carotid within the carotid sheath directly
Reveal Answer

A. The maxillary artery is one of the two terminal branches of the external carotid artery, arising deep to the neck of the mandible.

5. Why is the cerebral arterial circle clinically important in cerebral perfusion?
  • A. It can provide collateral flow if one contributing vessel becomes narrowed
  • B. It drains venous blood directly into the dural venous sinuses
  • C. It supplies all scalp tissues through superficial temporal branches only
  • D. It connects the carotid sheath directly to the cavernous sinus walls
Reveal Answer

A. The circle of Willis is important because it can maintain collateral cerebral perfusion when one part of the inflow is compromised.
